texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we consider the estimation of regression coefficients and signal-to-noise (SNR) ratio in high-dimensional Generalized Linear Models (GLMs), and explore their implications in inferring popular estimands such as average treatment effects in high dimensional observational studies. Under the 'proportional asymptotic' regime and Gaussian covariates with known (population) covariance Σ , we derive √ n -Consistent and Asymptotically Normal (CAN) estimators of our targets of inference through a Method-of-Moments type of estimators that bypasses estimation of high dimensional nuisance functions and hyperparameter tuning altogether. Additionally, under non-Gaussian covariates, we demonstrate universality of our results under certain additional assumptions on the regression coefficients and Σ . We also demonstrate that knowing Σ can be relaxed in our proposed methodology. Finally, we complement our theoretical results with extensive numerical experiments, in comparisons with competing methods.
texts.text: In this paper, we consider the estimation of regression coefficients and signal-to-noise (SNR) ratio in high-dimensional Generalized Linear Models (GLMs), and explore their implications in inferring popular estimands such as average treatment effects in high dimensional observational studies. Under the 'proportional asymptotic' regime and Gaussian covariates with known (population) covariance Σ , we derive √ n -Consistent and Asymptotically Normal (CAN) estimators of our targets of inference through a Method-of-Moments type of estimators that bypasses estimation of high dimensional nuisance functions and hyperparameter tuning altogether. Additionally, under non-Gaussian covariates, we demonstrate universality of our results under certain additional assumptions on the regression coefficients and Σ . We also demonstrate that knowing Σ can be relaxed in our proposed methodology. Finally, we complement our theoretical results with extensive numerical experiments, in comparisons with competing methods.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Statistical inference in Generalized Linear Models (GLMs) (Nelder and Wedderburn, 1972; McCullagh and Nelder, 1989), although a classical topic in statistics, has witnessed renewed enthusiasm in the modern big data era spurred by both theoretical and computational challenges that arise therein (Janková and van de Geer, 2018; Cai et al., 2023; Sur and Candès, 2019; Sur et al., 2019; Candès and Sur, 2020; Zhao et al., 2022). This line of research has in turn found resonance in the challenges encountered in the context of inference in observational studies (Chernozhukov et al., 2018; Athey et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2024; Yadlowsky, 2022; Celentano and Wainwright, 2023). Specifically, estimation of quantities like the causal effect of an exposure on an outcome or estimation of population quantities under missing data typically relies on understanding nuisance functions such as outcome regression and propensity scores (Robins et al., 1994; Scharfstein et al., 1999). These
texts.text: Statistical inference in Generalized Linear Models (GLMs) (Nelder and Wedderburn, 1972; McCullagh and Nelder, 1989), although a classical topic in statistics, has witnessed renewed enthusiasm in the modern big data era spurred by both theoretical and computational challenges that arise therein (Janková and van de Geer, 2018; Cai et al., 2023; Sur and Candès, 2019; Sur et al., 2019; Candès and Sur, 2020; Zhao et al., 2022). This line of research has in turn found resonance in the challenges encountered in the context of inference in observational studies (Chernozhukov et al., 2018; Athey et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2024; Yadlowsky, 2022; Celentano and Wainwright, 2023). Specifically, estimation of quantities like the causal effect of an exposure on an outcome or estimation of population quantities under missing data typically relies on understanding nuisance functions such as outcome regression and propensity scores (Robins et al., 1994; Scharfstein et al., 1999). These

texts.label: text
texts.orig: regression functions are often modeled as suitable GLMs, when one needs to adjust for confounders possibly larger in dimension than the available sample size. There now exists a dedicated and comprehensive methodology to deal with inference in both GLMs or observational studies with high dimensional covariates/confounders focused on ideas based on semiparametric theory (Zhang and Zhang, 2014; Javanmard and Montanari, 2014; Janková and van de Geer, 2018; Athey et al., 2018; Smucler et al., 2019; Bradic et al., 2019a,b; Tan, 2020a; Dukes and Vansteelandt, 2021; Wang and Shah, 2024; Liu et al., 2023; Su et al., 2023). Indeed, this class of methods, in turn, relies on rates of convergence for consistent estimators of high dimensional GLM parameters (Negahban et al., 2012). However, even the mere existence of such a consistent estimator relies on a priori unknown low-dimensional (such as sparsity) assumptions in respective GLMs (Verzelen, 2012; Collier et al., 2017; Cai and Guo, 2017; Bellec and Zhang, 2022).
texts.text: regression functions are often modeled as suitable GLMs, when one needs to adjust for confounders possibly larger in dimension than the available sample size. There now exists a dedicated and comprehensive methodology to deal with inference in both GLMs or observational studies with high dimensional covariates/confounders focused on ideas based on semiparametric theory (Zhang and Zhang, 2014; Javanmard and Montanari, 2014; Janková and van de Geer, 2018; Athey et al., 2018; Smucler et al., 2019; Bradic et al., 2019a,b; Tan, 2020a; Dukes and Vansteelandt, 2021; Wang and Shah, 2024; Liu et al., 2023; Su et al., 2023). Indeed, this class of methods, in turn, relies on rates of convergence for consistent estimators of high dimensional GLM parameters (Negahban et al., 2012). However, even the mere existence of such a consistent estimator relies on a priori unknown low-dimensional (such as sparsity) assumptions in respective GLMs (Verzelen, 2012; Collier et al., 2017; Cai and Guo, 2017; Bellec and Zhang, 2022).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To complement the above framework, recent times have witnessed a parallel focus to deal with cases when the entire GLM parameter vector cannot be estimated consistently, and yet there are potential low dimensional summaries of them that can yield themselves to desirable inferential strategies. As a byproduct, one can possibly provide reliable estimation in observational studies. One specific instance that has become popular is when the GLM parameters grow proportionally to the sample size in dimension and do not satisfy additional low-dimensional assumptions (Bean et al., 2013; El Karoui, 2013; Donoho and Montanari, 2016; Lei et al., 2018) 1 . To reflect the inherent difficulty of this setup in terms of the information-theoretic impossibility of estimating the GLM parameters consistently (Verzelen, 2012; Barbier et al., 2019), recent research has coined it as the 'inconsistency regime' (Celentano and Wainwright, 2023), and fundamental ideas have already started to carve the contours of this paradigm. A major theme of research in this regime often pertains to initial progress made under Gaussian covariates with known covariance (Bellec and Zhang, 2023) and subsequent demonstration of a universality principle (Zhao et al., 2022; Dudeja et al., 2023a; Han and Shen, 2023). Indeed, the Gaussian assumption is not necessarily a simplifying assumption in the development and analysis of the methods in this literature - one requires highly involved probabilistic machinery to produce sharp analyses of the derived estimators. Nevertheless, the assumption of Gaussianity, coupled with the knowledge or a sufficiently accurate estimator of the population (variance-)covariance matrix of baseline covariates inject enough structure and a priori information to simplify the process. Such structure and information enable one to bypass complicated estimators and analyses, while still achieving remarkably parallel guarantees for inference in GLMs and observational studies in the proportional asymptotic high-dimensional regime. The primary aim of this article is to take steps in that direction. Specifically, we demonstrate that for GLMs with link function meeting certain conditions (see Assumption L later), it is possible to construct a diffeomorphism between functionals of the GLM parameters and carefully crafted low-degree moments of the data, for which √ n -Consistent and Asymptotic Normal (CAN) estimators exist. This crucial observation forms the core of our proposed methodology.

Such structure and information enable one to bypass complicated estimators and analyses, while still achieving remarkably parallel guarantees for inference in GLMs and observational studies in the proportional asymptotic high-dimensional regime. texts.text: we refer readers to Lemma 1 later in the paper) for statistical functionals with nuisance models parameterized as high-dimensional GLMs with the dimension p proportional to n when the covariance matrix of the covariates are known. This allows the construction of estimators of relevant low-dimensional summaries of high-dimensional GLM parameters such as contrasts and Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR). texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (2) Our moment-based identification and estimation strategies generalize to parallel inferential techniques for popular objects of interest in observational studies such as average treatment effects and mean estimands under missing data - where the analyses depend on two nuisance functions modeled by high dimensional GLMs. Compared to the literature in this class of problems, we do not require sample-splitting and cross-fitting-based ideas owing to our ability to avoid estimating nuisance functions.
texts.text: Our moment-based identification and estimation strategies generalize to parallel inferential techniques for popular objects of interest in observational studies such as average treatment effects and mean estimands under missing data - where the analyses depend on two nuisance functions modeled by high dimensional GLMs. Compared to the literature in this class of problems, we do not require sample-splitting and cross-fitting-based ideas owing to our ability to avoid estimating nuisance functions.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (3) Our estimators completely bypass the estimation of high dimensional nuisance parameters and are CAN when the baseline covariates are Gaussian under some additional regularity conditions. We further demonstrate the universality of the proposal beyond Gaussian designs in terms of rates of convergence.
texts.text: Our estimators completely bypass the estimation of high dimensional nuisance parameters and are CAN when the baseline covariates are Gaussian under some additional regularity conditions. We further demonstrate the universality of the proposal beyond Gaussian designs in terms of rates of convergence.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (4) We also demonstrate that the assumption of knowing the population covariance matrix Σ of the design can be dropped for our proposal when the sample covariance matrix estimator of Σ is invertible and p < c · n for some constant c , under Gaussian designs.
texts.text: We also demonstrate that the assumption of knowing the population covariance matrix Σ of the design can be dropped for our proposal when the sample covariance matrix estimator of Σ is invertible and p < c · n for some constant c , under Gaussian designs.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (5) We conduct extensive numerical experiments to verify the validity of our proposals in finite sample, as well as comparing them with methods from the emerging recent literature. Readers can access the codes for replicating our numerical results from the accompanied GitHub repository.
texts.text: We conduct extensive numerical experiments to verify the validity of our proposals in finite sample, as well as comparing them with methods from the emerging recent literature. Readers can access the codes for replicating our numerical results from the accompanied GitHub repository.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our research draws inspiration from several past and ongoing research that aims to address inference in high-dimensional problems. To present a compact survey and comparison with the most related members of this literature we divide our discussions across three broad themes: inference in GLMs, inference for popular observational studies, and the knowledge of variance-covariance matrix of baseline covariates. In each of these sub-parts, we shall further briefly touch upon both ultra-high dimensional regimes under sparsity and proportional high dimensional regimes without sparsity aspects of the results in literature.
texts.text: Our research draws inspiration from several past and ongoing research that aims to address inference in high-dimensional problems. To present a compact survey and comparison with the most related members of this literature we divide our discussions across three broad themes: inference in GLMs, inference for popular observational studies, and the knowledge of variance-covariance matrix of baseline covariates. In each of these sub-parts, we shall further briefly touch upon both ultra-high dimensional regimes under sparsity and proportional high dimensional regimes without sparsity aspects of the results in literature.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the last two decades, statistical inference for linear and quadratic forms of high dimensional GLM parameters has attracted significant attention from the statistical research community (Verzelen, 2012; Zhang and Zhang, 2014; Javanmard and Montanari, 2014; Dicker, 2014; Verzelen and Gassiat, 2018; Cai and Guo, 2018; Collier et al., 2017; Guo and Cheng, 2022; Battey and Reid, 2023; Celentano and Montanari, 2024). Two complementary tracks of emphasis have emerged in this regard. In the first line of activities, the strategy of inference often draws inspiration from classical semiparametric theory (Janková and van de Geer, 2018) and requires the consistent estimation of ultra high-dimensional (when the dimension p is much larger than the sample size n ) GLM
texts.text: In the last two decades, statistical inference for linear and quadratic forms of high dimensional GLM parameters has attracted significant attention from the statistical research community (Verzelen, 2012; Zhang and Zhang, 2014; Javanmard and Montanari, 2014; Dicker, 2014; Verzelen and Gassiat, 2018; Cai and Guo, 2018; Collier et al., 2017; Guo and Cheng, 2022; Battey and Reid, 2023; Celentano and Montanari, 2024). Two complementary tracks of emphasis have emerged in this regard. In the first line of activities, the strategy of inference often draws inspiration from classical semiparametric theory (Janková and van de Geer, 2018) and requires the consistent estimation of ultra high-dimensional (when the dimension p is much larger than the sample size n ) GLM

texts.label: text
texts.orig: parameters - which need to rely on apriori low-dimensional assumptions, such as sparsity, on GLM parameter vectors (Collier et al., 2017; Cai and Guo, 2018; Cai et al., 2023). To explore regimes where the existence of consistent estimators of entire GLM parameter vectors are impossible, a complementary theme of inference in GLMs has sprung in the last decade under proportional asymptotics (when the dimension p is proportional to the sample size n ) (Sur and Candès, 2019; Sur et al., 2019; Candès and Sur, 2020; Zhao et al., 2022). In this regime, the strategy typically involves a careful debiasing surgery on initial suitable yet inconsistent GLM parameter vectors to yield sophisticated CAN estimators of linear and quadratic forms. Indeed, literature in this second direction is more recent and had initially focused on linear models in terms of (i) characterizing the precise risk behavior of convex regularized procedures - first for Gaussian covariates (see Bayati and Montanari (2011); Stojnic (2013); Thrampoulidis et al. (2018); Miolane and Montanari (2021); Celentano et al. (2023) and references therein) and then beyond Gaussian (Gerbelot et al., 2020, 2022; Li and Sur, 2023; Han and Shen, 2023); and (ii) inference of linear and quadratic forms of the parameter vector - albeit mainly in the regime where the design covariance is known apriori (Bellec and Zhang, 2022; Bellec, 2024; Bellec and Zhang, 2023; Song et al., 2024). Results for GLMs are more complete in terms of estimation of the whole parameter vector using convex regularized methods. Parallel methods in GLMs for inference on linear and quadratic forms are more recent, quite case-specific (e.g. consider binary regression with logistic and/or probit link), do not always cover the whole proportional regime (i.e. all aspects ratio considerations of p/n ) without further assumptions, and often yield coverage guarantees in an average sense on individual coordinates of the GLM parameter vector instead of individually across coordinates (Bellec, 2024) 2 . Another work related to ours is Sawaya et al. (2023), which also concerns statistical inference for GLM parameters. In particular, under assumptions (1) the link function having certain asymmetry (see Section A.8 of Sawaya et al. (2023) for a precise statement) and (2) the covariates X having zero mean, Sawaya et al. (2023) use only moments of Y to estimate certain quantities in the State Evolution system, that characterizes the asymptotic behavior of the maximum likelihood and its convex regularized analog, to conduct inference - thus obviating the requirement of knowing the population covariance matrix Σ of X or estimating Σ with sufficiently fast convergence rate. However, this important advantage is at the expense of precluding important GLMs such as the logistic or probit regression. Finally, the theoretical results of Sawaya et al. (2023) rely on assuming the existence and suitable boundedness of estimators based on minimizing possibly regularized GLM loss functions as well as the existence of unique positive solutions to relevant state evolution equations - which needs to be further verified and rested outside the scope of the work Sawaya et al. (2023). Since we bypass the estimation of the entire parameter vector while performing CAN estimation of low-dimensional summaries of them, our results do not rely on such further assumptions.

(2018); Miolane and Montanari (2021); Celentano et al. (2023) and references therein) and then beyond Gaussian (Gerbelot et al., 2020, 2022; Li and Sur, 2023; Han and Shen, 2023); and (ii) inference of linear and quadratic forms of the parameter vector - albeit mainly in the regime where the design covariance is known apriori (Bellec and Zhang, 2022; Bellec, 2024; Bellec and Zhang, 2023; Song et al., 2024). Results for GLMs are more complete in terms of estimation of the whole parameter vector using convex regularized methods. Parallel methods in GLMs for inference on linear and quadratic forms are more recent, quite case-specific (e.g. consider binary regression with logistic and/or probit link), do not always cover the whole proportional regime (i.e. all aspects ratio considerations of p/n ) without further assumptions, and often yield coverage guarantees in an average sense on individual coordinates of the GLM parameter vector instead of individually across coordinates (Bellec, 2024) 2 . In particular, under assumptions (1) the link function having certain asymmetry (see Section A.8 of Sawaya et al. (2023) for a precise statement) and (2) the covariates X having zero mean, Sawaya et al. (2023) use only moments of Y to estimate certain quantities in the State Evolution system, that characterizes the asymptotic behavior of the maximum likelihood and its convex regularized analog, to conduct inference - thus obviating the requirement of knowing the population covariance matrix Σ of X or estimating Σ with sufficiently fast convergence rate. However, this important advantage is at the expense of precluding important GLMs such as the logistic or probit regression. Finally, the theoretical results of Sawaya et al. (2023) rely on assuming the existence and suitable boundedness of estimators based on minimizing possibly regularized GLM loss functions as well as the existence of unique positive solutions to relevant state evolution equations - which needs to be further verified and rested outside the scope of the work Sawaya et al. (2023). Since we bypass the estimation of the entire parameter vector while performing CAN estimation of low-dimensional summaries of them, our results do not rely on such further assumptions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Quantities like average treatment effects and mean parameters in missing data problems have now emerged as quintessential examples of functionals in observational studies where the challenges of high dimensional baseline covariates require careful methodological consideration. Similar to the literature in GLM, two complementary themes have emerged here as well - one regarding ultrahigh-dimensional regimes under sparsity and another regarding proportional asymptotic regimes without sparsity but under known Gaussian covariate designs (Celentano and Wainwright, 2023)
texts.text: Quantities like average treatment effects and mean parameters in missing data problems have now emerged as quintessential examples of functionals in observational studies where the challenges of high dimensional baseline covariates require careful methodological consideration. Similar to the literature in GLM, two complementary themes have emerged here as well - one regarding ultrahigh-dimensional regimes under sparsity and another regarding proportional asymptotic regimes without sparsity but under known Gaussian covariate designs (Celentano and Wainwright, 2023)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: or for specific functionals with p < n (Yadlowsky, 2022; Jiang et al., 2024). Since the ultra-highdimensional regime under sparsity has been heavily studied (Athey et al., 2018; Smucler et al., 2019; Bradic et al., 2019a,b; Tan, 2020a,b; Dukes and Vansteelandt, 2021; Wang and Shah, 2024; Liu et al., 2023), the results therein are somewhat complete in terms of necessary and sufficient conditions for CAN estimation. However, without Gaussian covariates or the assumption that p < n , neither systematic methods nor CAN guarantees exist for the above examples. Our methods aim to fill this gap in the literature. Finally, we remark that our proposed estimators involve secondorder U -statistics, thus also drawing connections to the growing literature on using Higher-Order U -statistics in semiparametric problems in observational studies (Robins et al., 2008; van der Laan et al., 2021; Kennedy et al., 2024; Bonvini et al., 2024; Breunig and Chen, 2019, 2024). Also see Remark 14 of Section 3 for a more in-depth discussion.

Finally, we remark that our proposed estimators involve secondorder U -statistics, thus also drawing connections to the growing literature on using Higher-Order U -statistics in semiparametric problems in observational studies (Robins et al., 2008; van der Laan et al., 2021; Kennedy et al., 2024; Bonvini et al., 2024; Breunig and Chen, 2019, 2024). Also see Remark 14 of Section 3 for a more in-depth discussion.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The majority of our results relies on the knowledge of the variance-covariance matrix of baseline covariates in the study. This known (population) covariance assumption has also been consistently imposed in the literature on the inference of high-dimensional GLMs under proportional asymptotics (Bellec and Zhang, 2023; Bellec, 2024), in particular when p > n . Indeed, Verzelen and Gassiat (2018) demonstrate the impossibility of estimating or conducting statistical inference on certain functionals in high dimensional regression with unknown arbitrary variance-covariance matrix of the covariates when p /greatermuch n 1+ c for some c > 0. This does not preclude the designing of procedures informed by a priori assumptions on the variance-covariance matrix of the covariates - a philosophy that has indeed been successfully espoused in the ultra-high-dimensional sparse GLM-based inferences (Verzelen and Gassiat, 2018). Its parallels in the proportional asymptotic regime without sparsity assumptions are quite sporadic, and we are only aware of Li and Sur (2023), and to some extent Takahashi and Kabashima (2018), that address this problem for linear forms of the coefficients under the right-rotationally invariant design. Our main results also assume that Σ is known. However, under Gaussian designs, in Section 3, we establish √ n -consistency of our proposed estimator when Σ is unknown as long as the sample covariance matrix estimator of Σ is invertible, demonstrating that knowing Σ is not essential for our proposal. Furthermore, upon the completion of the first version of our draft, N. Verzelen brought to our attention Kong and Valiant (2018). In that paper, the authors developed an estimator of the quadratic form of the regression coefficients (also known as 'learnability' in the theoretical computer science literature) in logistic regression only when X ∼ N p ( 0 , Σ ) with p /equivasymptotic n , with and without knowing Σ . Their estimator without knowing Σ formally resembles the Higher-Order Influence Function estimators (Robins et al., 2008, 2016); we will discuss their similarity and difference further in Remark 14. Our results cover general GLMs beyond logistic regression without forcing the covariates to have zero mean. We also additionally consider more complex functionals often encountered in observational studies, such as the average treatment effects.

Indeed, Verzelen and Gassiat (2018) demonstrate the impossibility of estimating or conducting statistical inference on certain functionals in high dimensional regression with unknown arbitrary variance-covariance matrix of the covariates when p /greatermuch n 1+ c for some c > 0. This does not preclude the designing of procedures informed by a priori assumptions on the variance-covariance matrix of the covariates - a philosophy that has indeed been successfully espoused in the ultra-high-dimensional sparse GLM-based inferences (Verzelen and Gassiat, 2018). Its parallels in the proportional asymptotic regime without sparsity assumptions are quite sporadic, and we are only aware of Li and Sur (2023), and to some extent Takahashi and Kabashima (2018), that address this problem for linear forms of the coefficients under the right-rotationally invariant design. Our main results also assume that Σ is known. However, under Gaussian designs, in Section 3, we establish √ n -consistency of our proposed estimator when Σ is unknown as long as the sample covariance matrix estimator of Σ is invertible, demonstrating that knowing Σ is not essential for our proposal. Furthermore, upon the completion of the first version of our draft, N. Verzelen brought to our attention Kong and Valiant (2018). In that paper, the authors developed an estimator of the quadratic form of the regression coefficients (also known as 'learnability' in the theoretical computer science literature) in logistic regression only when X ∼ N p ( 0 , Σ ) with p /equivasymptotic n , with and without knowing Σ . Their estimator without knowing Σ formally resembles the Higher-Order Influence Function estimators (Robins et al., 2008, 2016); we will discuss their similarity and difference further in Remark 14. Our results cover general GLMs beyond logistic regression without forcing the covariates to have zero mean. We also additionally consider more complex functionals often encountered in observational studies, such as the average treatment effects.

texts.orig: To elaborate on the main thesis of the paper, we divide our discussions into the following subsections. In Section 2 we present our results on inference in GLMs followed by its applications in observational studies collected in Section 4. Subsequently, Section 5 validates the theoretical results via numerical experiments. Our article ends by discussing some open problems in Section 6. All the proof details

A general theme throughout this paper is to construct a multi-valued map Ψ = (Ψ 1 , · · · , Ψ k ) : D → R from its domain D to its range R using MoM. Given a subset I ⊂ [ k ], we let Ψ I := { Ψ j , j ∈ I } and Ψ -1 I be the inverse map of Ψ I if Ψ I is invertible. Given a k -th differentiable function f , let f ( k ) denote its k -th derivative. Given a subset I ⊂ [ k ], we let Ψ I := { Ψ j , j ∈ I } and Ψ -1 I be the inverse map of Ψ I if Ψ I is invertible. Given a k -th differentiable function f , let f ( k ) denote its k -th derivative. Finally, we denote ‖ f ‖ q and ‖ f ‖ ∞ as, respectively, the L q ( P )- and L ∞ -norms of f , for q ≥ 1.

Based on the Gaussian design and Stein's lemma, the moment equations (2) marry the moments on the LHS with certain nonlinear transformation of β on the RHS. The most important moment equation here is Ψ GLM 0 ,β induced by (3a), that maps the quadratic form γ 2 β to the moment m X Y, 2 . Assumption L on the link function and Assumption B on γ 2 β together ensures that Ψ GLM 0 ,β is a diffeomorphism, so γ 2 β = Ψ -1 GLM 0 ,β ( m X Y, 2 ) is identified. It will also be made clear later that Ψ GLM 0 ,β being a diffeomorphism entails that √ n -consistent and CAN estimators of γ 2 β can be constructed. Taking υ = e j , the j -th standard basis in R p , the same strategy identifies β j , for any j ∈ [ p ].

The most important moment equation here is Ψ GLM 0 ,β induced by (3a), that maps the quadratic form γ 2 β to the moment m X Y, 2 . Assumption L on the link function and Assumption B on γ 2 β together ensures that Ψ GLM 0 ,β is a diffeomorphism, so γ 2 β = Ψ -1 GLM 0 ,β ( m X Y, 2 ) is identified. It will also be made clear later that Ψ GLM 0 ,β being a diffeomorphism entails that √ n -consistent and CAN estimators of γ 2 β can be constructed. After identifying γ 2 β , by solving (3b), β /latticetop υ = m X Y, υ / f 1 ( γ 2 β ) is as well identified from the moments. texts.orig: The conclusions in Lemma 1, together with all the other identification results under Gaussian designs in this paper, do not require Assumption C . However, the above moment equations critically rely on the Gaussianity of X . It is natural to ask if, similar to a growing body of work studying universality for regression models under proportional asymptotics (see e.g. Bayati et al. (2015); Montanari and Saeed (2022); Montanari et al. (2023); Hu and Lu (2022); Dudeja et al. (2023b); Lahiry and Sur (2023) and references therein), one could move beyond Gaussian designs and demonstrate the universality of the above identification result. We provide a positive answer to this question following a relaxed identification criterion and shifting the burden of assumption from X to β .

We provide a positive answer to this question following a relaxed identification criterion and shifting the burden of assumption from X to β .

texts.orig: Definition 1 ( √ n -identifiability) . We say that a low-dimensional target parameter ψ ∈ R k , where k is strictly bounded, of the underlying statistical model P (e.g. Model GLM ) is √ n -identifiable if there exists a (possibly) nonlinear map Ψ from ψ to certain moments defined by P induced by ψ , such that if given two different values of the target parameter, ψ and ψ ′ , such that ‖ ψ -ψ ′ ‖ ≳ n -1 / 2 , then ‖ Ψ( ψ ) -Ψ( ψ ′ ) ‖ ≳ n -1 / 2 , for sufficiently large n .
texts.text: Definition 1 ( √ n -identifiability) . We say that a low-dimensional target parameter ψ ∈ R k , where k is strictly bounded, of the underlying statistical model P (e.g. Model GLM ) is √ n -identifiable if there exists a (possibly) nonlinear map Ψ from ψ to certain moments defined by P induced by ψ , such that if given two different values of the target parameter, ψ and ψ ′ , such that ‖ ψ -ψ ′ ‖ ≳ n -1 / 2 , then ‖ Ψ( ψ ) -Ψ( ψ ′ ) ‖ ≳ n -1 / 2 , for sufficiently large n .

The CAN property of the proposed MoM-based estimators relies on two separate results: (1) the CLT of first-order and second-order U -statistics, followed from the results in Bhattacharya and Ghosh (1992) (see Appendix E for a complete proof) (2) Ψ GLM 0 ,β is a diffeomorphism, and its inverse map, Ψ -1 GLM 0 ,β , has bounded derivative so the Delta Method can be applied. ■

texts.orig: Remark 7. In Proposition 2 (and similar results related to the CAN property of our proposed estimator in the sequel), we need some extra assumptions on the convergence of inner products such as β /latticetop f ( Σ ) β . It is worth mentioning that the assumption imposed in the main text might not be tight. By speculating the derivations in Appendix E, one only needs either β /latticetop Σ β or β /latticetop Σ 3 β to converge. But to avoid unnecessary technical complications that are irrelevant to the main theme of the paper, we decide not to pursue further in this direction. Also, one can easily find sufficient conditions to establish the convergence of such quantities. As a simple example, when Σ = I p and β satisfies Assumption U 0 , we immediately have β /latticetop f ( Σ ) β to converge.

texts.orig: Remark 8. At this point, readers might wonder why we consider moments such as m X Y, 2 that involves X . When µ ≡ 0 , Y ∈ { 0 , 1 } and φ ( · ) = expit ( · ), it is obvious that all the moments of Y reduce to E [ Y ] ≡ 0 . 5. Thus without leveraging information of X , it is generally impossible to identify functionals of β . This is why the moment-based estimator in Sawaya et al. (2023), based only on E [ Y ], cannot be directly applied to logistic or probit regression. ■

texts.orig: Next, we show that the zero covariate-mean condition is not essential to our moment-based approach, but a larger system of moment equations is required to identify relevant parameters in GLMs. The development closely mirrors that of the previous section when µ is known to equal 0 . As before, we first set the stage under Gaussian designs.

texts.orig: In the proof of the above theorem in Appendix E.2, we unpack the conditions of the theorem further. On a higher level, convergence of inner products 〈 v 1 , v 2 〉 f ( Σ ) to certain nontrivial limits relates to whether the asymptotic variances of moment estimators based on U -statistics, after scaled by √ n , converge to nontrivial limits. For statistical inference, one could construct standard Wald intervals by estimating the asymptotic variances in Theorem 2 by the bootstrap method developed in Liu et al. (2024). The performance of bootstrap variance estimators will be assessed in Appendix H.5.

texts.orig: Remark 11 (On the scalings between p and n ) . Even though we focus on the asymptotic regime where p scales proportionally with n , it is worth noting that our proposed estimators can achieve consistency even when p /greatermuch n , as long as p = o ( n 2 ). This is because the variances of our U -statisticbased moment estimators are generally of order 1 n ∨ p n 2 . Asymptotic normality also holds with a different scaling factor n/ √ p instead of n 1 / 2 , by applying results from Bhattacharya and Ghosh (1992). ■

(2023) only applies to asymmetric link functions because it does not rely on Σ . ■

Since κ is unknown, two different methods have been proposed: ProbeFrontier by Sur and Candès (2019) leveraging and SLOE by Yadlowsky et al. (2021) using a reparameterization of (9) in terms of p -1 ∑ p j =1 ̂ β 2 MLE ,j instead of κ . The proposed MoM-based method evidently offers another approach to estimating κ from data. This is also the problem that Sawaya et al. (2023) try to solve. However, as mentioned in the Introduction, the approach in Sawaya et al. (2023) only applies to asymmetric link functions because it does not rely on Σ . ■

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3 The Case of Unknown Population Covariance
texts.text: 3 The Case of Unknown Population Covariance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we demonstrate that knowing Σ is not essential to the proposed MoM method in the previous section. To ease presentation, we focus on the case of knowing µ ≡ 0 . We will discuss briefly in Appendix G.2.3 for completeness how to estimate Σ in the general case where both µ and Σ are unknown. In the accompanying GitHub repository, we also implement the proposed method for this more general scenario.
texts.text: In this section, we demonstrate that knowing Σ is not essential to the proposed MoM method in the previous section. To ease presentation, we focus on the case of knowing µ ≡ 0 . We will discuss briefly in Appendix G.2.3 for completeness how to estimate Σ in the general case where both µ and Σ are unknown. In the accompanying GitHub repository, we also implement the proposed method for this more general scenario.

texts.orig: Remark 14. ̂ m X Y, 2 defined in (10) with the prefactor ( n 2 -p -1) / n 2 removed is nothing but the empirical second-order influence function of the functional E [ Y X /latticetop ] Σ -1 E [ X Y ], to our knowledge first developed in Liu et al. (2017). In Liu et al. (2017), however, they did not make distributional assumptions on X such as Assumption G 0 or even Assumption G , resulting in more complicated higher-order U -statistics for correcting the bias due to estimating Σ by ˜ Σ . In particular, the order can diverge to infinity as n →∞ . Gaussian designs significantly simplify the diverging-order U -statistic estimator to a rescaled second-order U -statistic. An interesting question to further explore is which other types of assumptions on X , e.g. right rotationally invariant designs (Li and Sur, 2023), can also reduce higher-order U -statistics to simpler second-order U -statistics.

In particular, the order can diverge to infinity as n →∞ . Gaussian designs significantly simplify the diverging-order U -statistic estimator to a rescaled second-order U -statistic. An interesting question to further explore is which other types of assumptions on X , e.g. right rotationally invariant designs (Li and Sur, 2023), can also reduce higher-order U -statistics to simpler second-order U -statistics.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When p ≥ n , here we follow the higher-order U -statistic construction of Kong and Valiant (2018) to avoid estimating Σ -1 . Kong and Valiant (2018) also developed a polynomial-time algorithm for computing higher-order U -statistics, but only when the U -statistic kernels are symmetric. When µ is unknown, the involved U -statistics have asymmetric kernels (see Section 2.2 and Appendix G.2.3), making the computation a much more challenging problem that we plan to address in a separate paper. We mention in passing that Robins et al. (2016) also constructed higher-order U -statistics, with order diverging at rate √ log n , to estimate quantities such as m X Y, 2 . Their estimators, however, first estimate the density function of X and then estimate Σ -1 by numerical integration with respect to the estimated density function. Higher-order U -statistics are employed to reduce the bias due to density estimation, rather than approximating Σ -1 by polynomials. We leave the study of statistical implications of this subtle difference to a future paper. ■
texts.text: When p ≥ n , here we follow the higher-order U -statistic construction of Kong and Valiant (2018) to avoid estimating Σ -1 . Kong and Valiant (2018) also developed a polynomial-time algorithm for computing higher-order U -statistics, but only when the U -statistic kernels are symmetric. When µ is unknown, the involved U -statistics have asymmetric kernels (see Section 2.2 and Appendix G.2.3), making the computation a much more challenging problem that we plan to address in a separate paper. We mention in passing that Robins et al. (2016) also constructed higher-order U -statistics, with order diverging at rate √ log n , to estimate quantities such as m X Y, 2 . Their estimators, however, first estimate the density function of X and then estimate Σ -1 by numerical integration with respect to the estimated density function. Higher-order U -statistics are employed to reduce the bias due to density estimation, rather than approximating Σ -1 by polynomials. We leave the study of statistical implications of this subtle difference to a future paper. ■

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we appeal to the discussions in previous parts to develop estimators for some popular quantities arising in the context of observational studies. We only present theoretical results when the covariates under study have a Gaussian distribution with known covariance Σ . Results with unknown Σ can be derived similarly by following the arguments in Section 3. The universality of the proposed procedure can be established by analogous arguments in the proof of Lemma 4, and hence omitted to avoid repetition and simplify exposition. The examples we discuss here are popular members of what is now known as the class of Doubly-Robust Functionals (Robins et al., 2008; Rotnitzky et al., 2021; Chernozhukov et al., 2022) where in high dimensional instances of the problem practitioners often aim to model the two high dimensional relevant nuisance parameters of the underlying model through flexible GLMs. Although we consider specific and most popular examples in this class, a potential future research avenue is to extend the results in this section to the entire class of Doubly-Robust Functionals characterized in Rotnitzky et al. (2021).
texts.text: In this section, we appeal to the discussions in previous parts to develop estimators for some popular quantities arising in the context of observational studies. We only present theoretical results when the covariates under study have a Gaussian distribution with known covariance Σ . Results with unknown Σ can be derived similarly by following the arguments in Section 3. The universality of the proposed procedure can be established by analogous arguments in the proof of Lemma 4, and hence omitted to avoid repetition and simplify exposition. The examples we discuss here are popular members of what is now known as the class of Doubly-Robust Functionals (Robins et al., 2008; Rotnitzky et al., 2021; Chernozhukov et al., 2022) where in high dimensional instances of the problem practitioners often aim to model the two high dimensional relevant nuisance parameters of the underlying model through flexible GLMs. Although we consider specific and most popular examples in this class, a potential future research avenue is to extend the results in this section to the entire class of Doubly-Robust Functionals characterized in Rotnitzky et al. (2021).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We divide our discussions into three subsections: estimation of causal effects of binary treatment in linear structural models, estimating quantities under missing data, and estimating generalized covariance measure (or equivalently expected conditional covariance (Liu et al., 2024)) that have gained popularity in recent literature on conditional independence testing (Shah and Peters, 2020; Christgau et al., 2023). Throughout this section, we assume that we have access to n i.i.d. copies of the triples ( X i , A i , Y i ) n i =1 i . i . d . ∼ P where X is the covariates or the design matrix, except for Section 4.2 with a minor modification. The approaches considered in Section 3 to estimate Σ -1 from data apply to all examples here without any conceptual challenges. Consequently, we only present results assuming that Σ is known.

Throughout this section, we assume that we have access to n i.i.d. copies of the triples ( X i , A i , Y i ) n i =1 i . i . d . ∼ P where X is the covariates or the design matrix, except for Section 4.2 with a minor modification. The approaches considered in Section 3 to estimate Σ -1 from data apply to all examples here without any conceptual challenges. Consequently, we only present results assuming that Σ is known.

texts.orig: To our knowledge, the above is the first result for CAN estimation for average treatment effect type quantities under the proportional asymptotic regime where p > n is allowed. In this regard, Jiang et al. (2024); Yadlowsky (2022) operate under p < n regime, without knowing Σ , owing to their reliance on ordinary least squares type estimator for the outcome regression. Our results close this gap in the literature under the knowledge of Σ . The proofs of CAN for all the examples in Section 4, including Theorem 3, can be found in Appendix E. The CAN property of ̂ ψ is a corollary of the CAN property established for the estimators of the linear and quadratic forms in Theorem 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we verify our theory via extensive numerical experiments. We consider two types of problems. The first problem, studied in Section 5.1, is on estimating linear and quadratic forms of regression coefficients α of a GLM between the response A and baseline covariates X . The second problem, studied in Section 5.2, is on estimating the mean of a response Y subject to missingness under Model MAR . As mentioned, the latter problem is also isomorphic to estimating treatment specific mean from observational studies under no unmeasured confounding. All the results in the numerical experiments are based on 500 Monte Carlos. The dimension-sample size ratio p/n is fixed at 1.2 in Section 5.1 and 1.25 in Section 5.2. Appendix H contains additional simulation results that are complementary to those reported here in the main text. We always assume that Σ is known except that in Appendix H.4 we conduct simulations to evaluate the performance of our proposed estimators in Section 3.1 when knowing neither µ nor Σ . The bootstrap variance estimators mentioned right after Theorem 2 are assessed in Appendix H.5.
texts.text: In this section, we verify our theory via extensive numerical experiments. We consider two types of problems. The first problem, studied in Section 5.1, is on estimating linear and quadratic forms of regression coefficients α of a GLM between the response A and baseline covariates X . The second problem, studied in Section 5.2, is on estimating the mean of a response Y subject to missingness under Model MAR . As mentioned, the latter problem is also isomorphic to estimating treatment specific mean from observational studies under no unmeasured confounding. All the results in the numerical experiments are based on 500 Monte Carlos. The dimension-sample size ratio p/n is fixed at 1.2 in Section 5.1 and 1.25 in Section 5.2. Appendix H contains additional simulation results that are complementary to those reported here in the main text. We always assume that Σ is known except that in Appendix H.4 we conduct simulations to evaluate the performance of our proposed estimators in Section 3.1 when knowing neither µ nor Σ . The bootstrap variance estimators mentioned right after Theorem 2 are assessed in Appendix H.5.

texts.orig: · When the design is Gaussian (Settings 1 & 2, Figures 1, 2, 7 and 8), Figures 1 shows that our proposed MoM-based estimator of α 1 and α 100 generally have similar √ n × bias, variance, and mean squared error to the estimator proposed in Bellec (2024). Moreover, Figures 2 and 8 showcase the histograms and normal quantile-quantile plots of the U -statistic-based moment estimators and estimators of λ α , γ 2 α , α 1 and α 100 by solving the moment system (5). It is clear from these figures that the sampling distributions of both the U -statistic-based moment estimators and our estimators α 1 and γ 2 α are close to the Gaussian distribution, further confirming by our theoretical results on the GAN property of our proposed MoM-based estimators.
texts.text: When the design is Gaussian (Settings 1 & 2, Figures 1, 2, 7 and 8), Figures 1 shows that our proposed MoM-based estimator of α 1 and α 100 generally have similar √ n × bias, variance, and mean squared error to the estimator proposed in Bellec (2024). Moreover, Figures 2 and 8 showcase the histograms and normal quantile-quantile plots of the U -statistic-based moment estimators and estimators of λ α , γ 2 α , α 1 and α 100 by solving the moment system (5). It is clear from these figures that the sampling distributions of both the U -statistic-based moment estimators and our estimators α 1 and γ 2 α are close to the Gaussian distribution, further confirming by our theoretical results on the GAN property of our proposed MoM-based estimators.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · When the design is Rademacher (Settings 3 & 4, Figures 3, 4, 9 and 10), we observe similar results to those in the Gaussian settings. Therefore, under certain conditions on the design and the regression coefficients, our identification and estimation strategies based on Gaussian designs continue to be applicable - demonstrating the universality of our proposed procedure. Interestingly, the debiased estimator also exhibits universality, which deserves a further theoretical investigation. It is worth noting that although Setting 4 concerns sparse regression coefficients, the number of non-zero coefficients is large and the values of non-zero coefficients are the same, so numerically our MoM-based estimators work still fine. In Appendix H.2, we showcase a different simulation setting, in which only one coordinate of the coefficients is non-zero; there the results show that the Gaussian-based identification and estimation strategies no longer produce consistent estimators.

texts.orig: 5.2 Mean estimation with missing data under MAR
texts.text: 5.2 Mean estimation with missing data under MAR

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the problem of estimating the mean of a response Y under Model MAR , we also consider several different settings. We first describe the common part of the DGP: the data is generated according to Model MAR , with X i i . i . d . ∼ N p ( 0 , I p ) for i = 1 , · · · , n . We take E [ A | X ] = 0 . 1 + 0 . 9 · expit ( X /latticetop α ), as in Celentano and Wainwright (2023). Here the true value of the target parameter ψ is 0 because X has mean zero. We take the outcome noise ε ∼ N(0 , σ 2 ) with σ = 0 . 2. The sample size varies from 100 to 10000. We compare our proposed MoM-based estimator ̂ ψ with the two estimators proposed in Celentano and Wainwright (2023), respectively termed as 'Oracle ASCW' and 'Empirical SCA'. For these alternative estimators, initial estimates based on Ridge are also used, With the tuning parameter λ taking 50 values ranging from [0 . 01 , 10] with equal steps on a logarithmic scale. We refer readers to Celentano and Wainwright (2023) for the details of these two alternative estimators; here we simply implement the R code provided in the GitHub repository provided by the authors of Celentano and Wainwright (2023).

texts.orig: As can be seen from Figures 5 and 15, the MoM-based estimators generally have lower n × bias, variance, and mean squared error than the two estimators proposed in Celentano and Wainwright (2023), over a range of values of the tuning parameter λ , regardless of the configurations of the regression coefficients. Figures 6 and 16 display the histograms and normal quantile-quantile plots of the U -statistic-based moment estimators and the estimators of the target parameter ψ , together with λ α , γ 2 α , γ α,β (the byproducts of the system (12)). It is clear from these figures that the sampling distributions of both the U -statistic-based moment estimators and of the estimators of the target parameter ψ , together with λ α , γ 2 α , γ α,β (the byproducts of the system (12)), are close to the Gaussian distribution.

texts.orig: This paper proposes Method-of-Moments (MoM) estimators of functionals of the regression coefficients in high-dimensional GLMs under the proportional asymptotic regime, in the most part assuming Gaussian designs with known population covariance matrix Σ , following the current trend of literature. We demonstrate promising theoretical and numerical results about the MoM estimators. However, we emphasize that a more delicate comparison between our proposed approach

texts.orig: A problem that is worth pursuing in future work relates to the potentially non-Gaussian limiting distribution. When ‖ β ‖ is small, the corresponding U -statistic estimator may have positive probability of being negative and the truncation used in (4) could lead to non-Gaussian limiting distributions of ̂ γ 2 β . It will be an interesting problem to investigate whether the bootstrap approach also delivers asymptotically valid inference in such a setting.

When ‖ β ‖ is small, the corresponding U -statistic estimator may have positive probability of being negative and the truncation used in (4) could lead to non-Gaussian limiting distributions of ̂ γ 2 β . It will be an interesting problem to investigate whether the bootstrap approach also delivers asymptotically valid inference in such a setting.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we mostly assume that Σ is known, an assumption ubiquitously adopted in the current literature (see Section 1.2). Progress can be made when Σ is unknown, but under specific conditions; see e.g. Section 3 and Appendix G. It will be of greater interest to examine the case if we weaken the assumptions on Σ or X imposed in Section 3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Compared to GLMs, isotonic Single-Index Models (SIMs) offer a more flexible modeling option as they allow the link function to be unknown and nonparametric. To the best of our knowledge, Bellec (2024) is among the first to systematically study inference of functionals of regression coefficients in SIMs under proportional asymptotics. It is interesting to extend our framework to single-index models but it requires estimating the link function and hence higher-order moments, a complication that we plan to address in a separate paper. Another direction is to investigate if similar results for ATE still hold under the assumptions of Su et al. (2023), where only the propensity score is modeled by high-dimensional logistic regression but the outcome regression model is arbitrary. Finally, whenever parametric models such as GLMs are used, model misspecification is of potential concerns. The semiparametric partially nonlinear regression parameters considered in Vansteelandt and Dukes (2022), which reduce to functionals of regression coefficients when GLMs are correctly specified, can be another promising future avenue for our work.
texts.text: Compared to GLMs, isotonic Single-Index Models (SIMs) offer a more flexible modeling option as they allow the link function to be unknown and nonparametric. To the best of our knowledge, Bellec (2024) is among the first to systematically study inference of functionals of regression coefficients in SIMs under proportional asymptotics. It is interesting to extend our framework to single-index models but it requires estimating the link function and hence higher-order moments, a complication that we plan to address in a separate paper. Another direction is to investigate if similar results for ATE still hold under the assumptions of Su et al. (2023), where only the propensity score is modeled by high-dimensional logistic regression but the outcome regression model is arbitrary. Finally, whenever parametric models such as GLMs are used, model misspecification is of potential concerns. The semiparametric partially nonlinear regression parameters considered in Vansteelandt and Dukes (2022), which reduce to functionals of regression coefficients when GLMs are correctly specified, can be another promising future avenue for our work.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As our proposed MoM-based estimators critically rely on inverting a nonlinear map (generally without an analytic form), particularly in Appendix D, we also need the following result that is a consequence of the Inverse Function Theorem and a result believed to be due to Hadamard (Theorem 6.2.8 of Krantz and Parks (2002)).
texts.text: As our proposed MoM-based estimators critically rely on inverting a nonlinear map (generally without an analytic form), particularly in Appendix D, we also need the following result that is a consequence of the Inverse Function Theorem and a result believed to be due to Hadamard (Theorem 6.2.8 of Krantz and Parks (2002)).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma 9 (Hadamard Global Inverse Function Theorem) . Suppose R and M are smooth, simply connected open sets of R k for some positive integer k and Ψ : R → M is a twice-differentiable and proper map. If the Jacobian ∇ Ψ has determinant bounded away from 0 over R , then Ψ is a diffeomorphism.
texts.text: Lemma 9 (Hadamard Global Inverse Function Theorem) . Suppose R and M are smooth, simply connected open sets of R k for some positive integer k and Ψ : R → M is a twice-differentiable and proper map. If the Jacobian ∇ Ψ has determinant bounded away from 0 over R , then Ψ is a diffeomorphism.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. By Theorem 6.2.8 of Krantz and Parks (2002), since R and M are smooth, simply connected open sets of R k and Ψ is proper and has non-singular Jacobian, Ψ is a homeomorphism and hence bijective. By the Inverse Function Theorem, since the Jacobian ∇ Ψ has determinant bounded away from zero, Ψ is a local diffeomorphism. A bijective map that is a local diffeomorphism is a (global) diffeomorphism.
texts.text: Proof. By Theorem 6.2.8 of Krantz and Parks (2002), since R and M are smooth, simply connected open sets of R k and Ψ is proper and has non-singular Jacobian, Ψ is a homeomorphism and hence bijective. By the Inverse Function Theorem, since the Jacobian ∇ Ψ has determinant bounded away from zero, Ψ is a local diffeomorphism. A bijective map that is a local diffeomorphism is a (global) diffeomorphism.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Taking x ∈ R . Ψ is a proper map if x → ∂ R implies that Ψ( x ) → ∂ M : see Definition 6.2.2 of Krantz and Parks (2002) and discussions thereafter.
texts.text: Taking x ∈ R . Ψ is a proper map if x → ∂ R implies that Ψ( x ) → ∂ M : see Definition 6.2.2 of Krantz and Parks (2002) and discussions thereafter.

texts.orig: H.2 A simulation setting where universality does not hold
texts.text: H.2 A simulation setting where universality does not hold

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As we mentioned in Remark 10, the delocalization of regression coefficients or conditions alike shall be necessary to establish universality. Therefore, in this section, we conduct numerical experiments where Assumption U (2) or Assumption U 0 (2) is violated and demonstrate that the identification strategy based on Gaussian design indeed fails to deliver the correct estimates of the quadratic form or a single coordinate. To this end, we consider the DGP in which X i,j i . i . d . ∼ Rad(1 / 2), and α = (1 , 0 , · · · , 0) /latticetop .
texts.text: As we mentioned in Remark 10, the delocalization of regression coefficients or conditions alike shall be necessary to establish universality. Therefore, in this section, we conduct numerical experiments where Assumption U (2) or Assumption U 0 (2) is violated and demonstrate that the identification strategy based on Gaussian design indeed fails to deliver the correct estimates of the quadratic form or a single coordinate. To this end, we consider the DGP in which X i,j i . i . d . ∼ Rad(1 / 2), and α = (1 , 0 , · · · , 0) /latticetop .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results are summarized in Figures 11 and 12 below. It is clear that our MoM estimators of the quadratic form γ 2 α ≡ α /latticetop Σ α and the single coordinate α 1 are quite different from the true target parameters. For example, speculating Figure 12, the histogram of ̂ α 1 over 500 Monte Carlos does not even cover the true value of α 1 ; similarly, the true value of γ 2 α is on the edge of the histogram of ̂ γ 2 α over 500 Monte Carlos. Interestingly, when λ is relatively large, the debiased estimator of Bellec (2024) has √ n × bias close to 0, in which case the estimated regression coefficients are mostly very small. It will be interesting to further investigate the effect of λ on the bias, variance, and mean squared error of the debiased estimators.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: H.4 Simulations knowing neither µ nor Σ
texts.text: H.4 Simulations knowing neither µ nor Σ

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we further examine the finite sample performance of the proposed moment-based estimators for linear and quadratic forms of GLM parameters under varying knowledge of µ and Σ , with p < n/ 2. We focus exclusively on the case of dense regression coefficients and Gaussian designs. We split the sample into two non-overlapping subsamples I 1 and I 2 with equal size. We estimate Σ from I 1 and then estimate the moments with U -statistics from I 2 ; afterwards we reverse the roles of I 1 and I 2 and implement cross-fitting. Finally we use the cross-fitted moment estimator to estimate the linear form α /latticetop µ and the quadratic form α /latticetop Σ α of the regression coefficients.
texts.text: In this section, we further examine the finite sample performance of the proposed moment-based estimators for linear and quadratic forms of GLM parameters under varying knowledge of µ and Σ , with p < n/ 2. We focus exclusively on the case of dense regression coefficients and Gaussian designs. We split the sample into two non-overlapping subsamples I 1 and I 2 with equal size. We estimate Σ from I 1 and then estimate the moments with U -statistics from I 2 ; afterwards we reverse the roles of I 1 and I 2 and implement cross-fitting. Finally we use the cross-fitted moment estimator to estimate the linear form α /latticetop µ and the quadratic form α /latticetop Σ α of the regression coefficients.
